context.description: A large, mature US-based automotive OEM with deep lean/TPS-influenced culture. The supply chain function employs approximately 350 people across multiple sub-functions including procurement, supplier quality, logistics, production planning, inventory management, and supply chain strategy. The organization operates with formal governance cadences and structured decision-making processes typical of Japanese-influenced manufacturing culture adapted to US operations.

organisational_dynamics.culture: Process-driven, lean methodology embedded, consensus-oriented but hierarchical. Strong emphasis on standardization and continuous improvement.
organisational_dynamics.structure: Multi-layered with VP/Director/Manager/Specialist tiers across six core sub-functions. Regional plant-level supply chain teams report into central supply chain leadership.
organisational_dynamics.known_tensions: None specifically identified. This is a diagnostic baseline exercise to understand the current state of decision-making.

interview_objectives.research_topic: Decision-making topology of the supply chain function: mapping who makes what decisions, in what forums, with what authority, tied to what KPIs.
key_questions: What is this person's role and scope of responsibility?
Who do they report to and who reports to them?
What KPIs do they personally own or are accountable for?
What decisions do they make independently vs. escalate?
What recurring meetings do they attend?
What is the purpose of each meeting, who else attends, and what types of decisions are made there?
What are the key outputs or outcomes of those meetings?
How do decisions from meetings translate into action — who carries them out?
themes_to_probe: Formal decision authority vs. informal influence
KPI ownership clarity — sole owner vs. shared accountability
Meeting effectiveness — are decisions actually made or just discussed?
Cross-functional handoffs — where does supply chain decision-making intersect with manufacturing, engineering, finance?
Escalation pathways — what goes up and what gets resolved locally

interview_output_structure.format: Structured, punchy summaries per interviewee — no more than 2 pages each

required_topics.details: Title, sub-function, scope, reporting line (up and down)
List of KPIs owned or co-owned, how they are measured, frequency of review
Key decisions this person makes, decision boundaries, what gets escalated and to whom
List of recurring meetings attended, their cadence, attendees, purpose, and types of decisions made in each
What outputs or actions come from each meeting, who is accountable for follow-through

interview_output_structure.depth_criteria: Focused and factual. Prioritize structure and specificity over narrative. Each interview should produce data points that can be directly mapped onto the network diagram.
interview_output_structure.per_interview_deliverable: A structured role card with: role profile, KPI table, decision inventory, and meeting-by-meeting breakdown with attendees and decision types

report_objectives.report_type: Executive diagnostic brief with visual decision-making map
report_objectives.target_audience: Top management and consulting team — people who need to quickly understand the current state and identify priorities for improving decision-making

output_sections.description: Visual network diagram showing roles (nodes), meetings (nodes), KPIs (nodes), and the connections between them. Should show who attends what, who owns what, and where decision authority sits. Color-coded by sub-function and layered by seniority.

A structured overview of all core recurring meetings in the supply chain function: purpose, cadence, attendees by role, decision types, and outputs. Organized from strategic (quarterly/monthly) to operational (weekly/daily).
A matrix mapping every identified KPI to its owner(s), the meetings where it is reviewed, and the decisions it drives.

A layered view showing what types of decisions are made at each level (VP, Director, Manager, Specialist) and in which forums — highlighting where authority is concentrated, distributed, or ambiguous.

A punchy 2-3 page written brief summarizing: how decisions actually flow in the supply chain, where the system is clear and well-governed, where there are potential gaps, overlaps, or bottlenecks in decision-making, and a prioritized list of areas to investigate further for improvement.

focus_areas: Clarity of decision ownership across the function
Meeting effectiveness — are the right people in the right rooms making the right decisions?
KPI alignment — do KPIs drive the right decisions at the right level?
Cross-functional seams — where supply chain decision-making depends on or conflicts with adjacent functions
Structural efficiency — is the meeting and governance architecture proportionate or bloated?

synthetic_audience.design_rationale: Selected to cover all six sub-functions at multiple seniority levels, ensuring both strategic and operational decision-making is captured. Includes the VP of Supply Chain as the apex, directors who run sub-functions, managers who own day-to-day decisions, and key senior specialists who execute and attend operational meetings.

interviewees.rationale: Apex of the function. Sets strategy, chairs senior governance meetings, owns top-level KPIs, interfaces with C-suite and plant GMs.
Owns sourcing strategy for production materials — the largest spend category. Key decision-maker on supplier selection and cost targets.
Owns supplier performance standards and development programs. Gatekeeper for supplier approvals and quality escalations.
Owns inbound/outbound logistics network, freight spend, carrier management. Critical for on-time delivery KPIs.
Owns the master production schedule, balances demand with capacity. Central node connecting supply chain to manufacturing.
Owns long-range planning, network optimization, and data/reporting infrastructure. Provides the analytical backbone for strategic decisions.
Manages non-production spend (MRO, services, tooling). Different governance and decision cadence from direct procurement.
Manages a critical commodity category. Makes day-to-day sourcing decisions, negotiates contracts, manages supplier relationships.
Manages the most volatile and strategically sensitive commodity. Semiconductor shortages have elevated this role's decision-making importance.
Leads the team that conducts supplier audits, manages PPAPs, and handles containment actions. Key operational decision-maker on quality holds.
Runs improvement programs with underperforming suppliers. Decides on intervention strategies and resource allocation.
Manages milk runs, consolidation centers, and inbound carrier network. Owns inbound freight cost and on-time delivery to plants.
Manages finished vehicle and parts distribution. Different decision rhythm — tied to dealer network and customer delivery.
Translates demand plans into weekly/daily production schedules. Makes high-frequency decisions on sequencing and line balancing.
Manages material requirements planning, release schedules to suppliers, and shortage escalations. Critical operational node.
Owns inventory targets (days on hand), warehouse operations, and cycle count accuracy. Balances cost vs. availability.
Facilitates the Sales & Operations Planning process — a critical cross-functional governance meeting. Bridges demand and supply sides.
Monitors supply disruptions, manages risk mitigation playbooks. Increasingly important role post-COVID. Owns risk KPIs and escalation protocols.
Builds and maintains the KPI dashboards and scorecards used in governance meetings. Understands the data layer of decision-making.

interviewees.role: Plant Supply Chain Manager (Lead Assembly Plant)
interviewees.rationale: Represents the plant-level perspective. Manages local supply chain execution, attends both plant-level and central meetings. Critical for understanding how central decisions land on the ground.
